text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 7e910206bda6 title: Un nouveau modèle d' intelligence artificielle fait sensation: Kimi K1 summary: Un nouveau modèle d' intelligence artificielle fait sensation: Kimi K1.5, développé par Moonshot AI, se positionne comme un sérieux concurrent face à des géants tels qu' OpenAI et DeepSeek. content: Un nouveau modèle d' intelligence artificielle fait sensation: Kimi K1.5, développé par Moonshot AI, se positionne comme un sérieux concurrent face à des géants tels qu' OpenAI et DeepSeek. Ce modèle multimodal, capable de traiter à la fois texte et images, se distingue par ses performances et son approche innovante. Selon plusieurs sources, Kimi K1.5 ne se contente pas d' extraire du texte à partir d' images, mais analyse le contenu visuel en profondeur pour en tirer des informations pertinentes. Il se distingue également par sa mémoire étendue, capable de traiter des informations sur 200 000 caractères, dix fois plus que les modèles traditionnels, et d' effectuer des recherches en temps réel sur plus de 1 000 sites web. En termes de performance, Kimi K1.5 dépasse plusieurs autres modèles d' IA sur des benchmarks clés, avec des scores impressionnants sur des tests de raisonnement avancé, de mathématiques et de programmation. Il s' illustre notamment avec un score de 96,2 sur MATH-500 et une place dans le 94e percentile sur Codeforces, un test de programmation. Ce modèle n' est pas seulement performant sur le plan technique: il montre également une capacité remarquable à fournir des analyses boursières en croisant des données sur les tendances des marchés et les actualités récentes. Pour les développeurs, il offre une aide précieuse en matière de débogage en temps réel. date: 2025-02-17 date_precision: day category: innovation entities.startups[0].name: Kimi K1.5 entities.startups[0].country: Tunisia entities.startups[0].sector: Artificial Intelligence source_url: origin_file: articles.json

metadata.name: Un nouveau modèle d' intelligence artificielle fait sensation: Kimi K1

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 20f7ae56484c title: La startup kenyane Dojah offre une infrastructure anti-fraude permettant aux entreprises de détecter les fraudes, de surveiller les transactions et d' assurer leur conformité aux normes AML (anti-blanchiment d' argent) summary: La startup kenyane Dojah offre une infrastructure anti-fraude permettant aux entreprises de détecter les fraudes, de surveiller les transactions et d' assurer leur conformité aux normes AML (anti-blanchiment d' argent). content: La startup kenyane Dojah offre une infrastructure anti-fraude permettant aux entreprises de détecter les fraudes, de surveiller les transactions et d' assurer leur conformité aux normes AML (anti-blanchiment d' argent). Fondée en janvier 2021 par Tobi Ololade et Ayomide Oso, anciens de Y Combinator, Dojah se spécialise dans les solutions de vérification d' identité tout-en-un, facilitant les interactions des entreprises africaines avec des utilisateurs authentiques et leur accès à davantage de services. Les outils de Dojah couvrent l' ensemble du parcours client, de l' acquisition à la fidélisation, en passant par la surveillance des fraudes et l' amélioration des niveaux d' accès, tout en offrant une plateforme unique pour gérer l' ensemble de ces processus. Dojah aide les entreprises numériques et financières à garantir leur sécurité, à croître de manière fluide et à respecter les régulations AML, en simplifiant l' intégration des utilisateurs et en automatisant les vérifications nécessaires pour prévenir les fraudes et les vols d' identité. Face à une montée des fraudes et à des systèmes de vérification d' identité peu efficaces en Afrique, Dojah se distingue par son approche intégrée qui offre à la fois la prévention des fraudes et la vérification d' identité. Parmi ses concurrents figurent Smile ID, Prembly et Sift, mais Dojah se démarque par son expertise locale et l' utilisation de l' IA pour la détection des fraudes. Jusqu' à présent, la startup a levé 1,25 million de dollars et vérifié 50 millions d' identités, tout en élargissant ses capacités de détection de fraudes. Dojah opére actuellement au Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya et Afrique du Sud, et prévoit d' étendre ses services à d' autres pays africains tout en développant sa base d' utilisateurs grêce à une adoption croissante de ses solutions de surveillance en temps réel et de conformité AML. Le modèle économique de Dojah repose sur un SaaS B2B, avec des solutions tarifées par API et des plans d' abonnement. L' entreprise connaît une adoption forte et continue de croître, en mettant l' accent sur la scalabilité et l' expansion de son marché. date: 2025-02-17 date_precision: day category: startup entities.startups[0].name: Dojah entities.startups[0].country: Kenya funding.amount: 1250000 funding.currency: USD source_url: origin_file: articles.json

metadata.name: La startup kenyane Dojah offre une infrastructure anti-fraude permettant aux entreprises de détecter les fraudes, de surveiller les transactions et d' assurer leur conformité aux normes AML (anti-blanchiment d' argent)

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 8b05356d32d0 title: WEPAY, startup tunisienne spécialisée dans la PayTech, annonce le lancement officiel de TAPPY, la première solution Soft POS en Tunisie summary: WEPAY, a Tunisian PayTech startup, announces the official launch of TAPPY, the first Soft POS solution in Tunisia. content: WEPAY, startup tunisienne spécialisée dans la PayTech, annonce le lancement officiel de TAPPY, la première solution Soft POS en Tunisie. Basée sur la technologie Tap-To-Pay. Cette solution transforme les Smartphones et tablettes Android en terminaux de paiement sans contact, offrant ainsi une alternative innovante aux TPE physiques. Grâce à TAPPY, les commerçants et entreprises peuvent désormais accepter les paiements sans contact par carte bancaire sans avoir à investir dans un TPE traditionnel. Cette solution révolutionne le marché en supprimant les contraintes de location, de maintenance et de gestion des terminaux physiques. Disponible en quatre langues (français, arabe, dialecte tunisien et anglais), TAPPY propose une interface intuitive permettant: - La saisie du montant et l' acceptation de paiement sans contact. - L' annulation de transaction en cas de besoin. - La génération et le partage de reçus de paiement. - L' accès à un historique détaillé des transactions. Un kit de paiement est également disponible pour intégration aux solutions mobiles et systèmes de gestion de caisse, offrant ainsi une synchronisation automatisée des transactions."Nous sommes fiers de démarrer 2025 avec la mise en production de TAPPY, une solution qui enrichit l' offre de paiement digital en Tunisie" déclare Welaa Naima Moula, CEO de WEPAY. Et d' ajouter"Ce lancement positionne WEPAY en tant que leader de l' innovation des services de paiement, promouvant l' inclusion financière et contribuant à la réduction de l' utilisation du cash". Déjà adoptée par plusieurs acteurs du secteur bancaire et des paiements, TAPPY enregistre ses premiers paiements en production avec des retours clients très positifs. Le lancement de TAPPY a été précédé par une phase de certifications internationales et d' homologation conformément aux exigences et réglementations du marché tunisien. La solution garantit ainsi un haut niveau de sécurité et de conformité pour les transactions électroniques. date: 2025-02-17 date_precision: day category: startup entities.startups[0].name: WEPAY entities.startups[0].country: Tunisia entities.startups[0].sector: PayTech source_url: origin_file: articles.json

metadata.name: WEPAY, startup tunisienne spécialisée dans la PayTech, annonce le lancement officiel de TAPPY, la première solution Soft POS en Tunisie

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 8ddf4e3db02a title: Pixii Motors, une startup tunisienne pionnière dans le secteur de la mobilité électrique, fait partie des quatre jeunes entreprises africaines à bénéficier d' un investissement de 800 000$ de la part de Madica, une société d' investissement axée sur l' summary: Pixii Motors, une startup tunisienne pionnière dans le secteur de la mobilité électrique, fait partie des quatre jeunes entreprises africaines à bénéficier d' un investissement de 800 000$ de la part de Madica, une société d' investissement axée sur l' Afrique. content: Pixii Motors, une startup tunisienne pionnière dans le secteur de la mobilité électrique, fait partie des quatre jeunes entreprises africaines à bénéficier d' un investissement de 800 000$ de la part de Madica, une société d' investissement axée sur l' Afrique. Fondée sur la volonté de révolutionner la mobilité urbaine, Pixii Motors propose des véhicules électriques respectueux de l' environnement, adaptés aux besoins spécifiques des citadins à la recherche d' une alternative durable aux moyens de transport traditionnels. L' entreprise tunisienne, qui met l' accent sur la réduction de l' empreinte carbone, s' engage à offrir des solutions de mobilité à la fois innovantes et accessibles. Les autres startups africaines qui ont bénéficié de ce financement sont: Medikea (Tanzanie), Motherbeing (Égypte), et ToumAI (Maroc). Cet investissement se répartit équitablement à hauteur de 200 000$ par startup. En outre, chaque startup bénéficiera également d' un accompagnement personnalisé et de voyages d' immersion dans des écosystèmes technologiques locaux et mondiaux. L' initiative de Madica, lancée en décembre 2022 par le capital-risqueur Flourish Ventures, s' inscrit dans une dynamique d' investissement en Afrique où les startups émergentes attirent de plus en plus d' attention. Madica, qui a investi jusqu' à présent 1,6 million de dollars dans huit startups, prévoit de consacrer 6 millions de dollars à 30 autres entreprises d' ici 2025. Son objectif est de soutenir des entreprises avec des produits viables, une équipe fondatrice à temps plein et une forte traction sur le marché. Ce qui distingue particulièrement Madica, c' est son engagement pour la diversité de genre au sein de ses startups. Emmanuel Adegboye, responsable de Madica, souligne dans un communiqué que l' entreprise dépasse déjà son objectif de 50% de diversité de genre dans les équipes dirigeantes, un modèle inclusif qui se reflète également dans la part importante de PDG féminins dans son portefeuille. date: 2025-02-13 date_precision: day category: startup ecosystem entities.startups[0].name: Pixii Motors entities.startups[0].country: Tunisia entities.startups[0].sector: Mobility entities.startups[1].name: Medikea entities.startups[1].country: Tanzania entities.startups[2].name: Motherbeing entities.startups[2].country: Egypt entities.startups[3].name: ToumAI entities.startups[3].country: Morocco funding.amount: 800000 funding.currency: USD fu

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 734949947690 title: ICC Tunisia organise l'Innovation Summit 2025 au profit des startups tunisiennes summary: La Chambre internationale de commerce a organisé l'Innovation Summit 2025 en collaboration avec Terna Innovation Zone et Mind The Bridge. content: En collaboration avec le projet d'accompagnement des startups Terna Innovation Zone et Mind The Bridge, la Chambre internationale de commerce a organisé aujourd'hui, dans un hôtel à Tunis, l'événement Innovation Summit 2025 sous le slogan «la Tunisie, le hub régional de l'innovation ouverte». Cette rencontre a révélé un Call for Applications pour choisir cinq startups en matière d'innovation ouverte. Le sommet a réuni des startuppeurs tunisiens, des universitaires et des décideurs pour discuter de divers sujets intéressants, à l'instar de la protection de la propriété intellectuelle dans l'ére de l'IA, les moyens de financement des startups et la Tunisie comme un pont entre l'Europe et l'Afrique. Mind The Bridge est un projet d'accompagnement et de connexion de startups en Afrique, notamment en Afrique du Sud, au Kenya, en Égypte et au Nigeria. Cette fois, Mind The Bridge a choisi la Tunisie comme cinquième destination pour lancer une plateforme pour l'innovation ouverte. Le débat était une opportunité pour annoncer la publication du rapport «Tech Scaleup Tunisia - 2025», qui souligne les avancées de la Tunisie comme hub technologique en Afrique du Nord, avec 17 scaleups et des initiatives comme le Startup Act. Malgré des succès comme Instadeep, le financement reste un défi, avec seulement 0,2 milliard de dollars levés. Le sommet était un rendez-vous important pour soutenir cet écosystème en développement. Le choix de la Tunisie est justifié par l'ICC par le potentiel des jeunes startupeurs tunisiens. Selon Mouna Chaieb, la présidente de la commission économie numérique de l'ICC, la Tunisie est le 5ème pays exportateur d'ingénieurs dans le monde, 45ème en nombre de chercheurs et 50ème en résultats scientifiques et écologiques des étudiants. Ces indicateurs prouvent la richesse humaine en Tunisie, malgré les lacunes économiques et sociales. date: 2025-12-02 date_precision: day category: startup ecosystem source_url: origin_file: articles.json

metadata.name: ICC Tunisia organise l'Innovation Summit 2025 au profit des startups tunisiennes

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 47b0aa9ba00e title: La startup tunisienne Rybsen, spécialisée dans le recyclage des eaux industrielles, a franchi une étape importante avec l' installation de sa première machine AquaClean en Afrique summary: La startup tunisienne Rybsen a installé sa première machine AquaClean en Afrique, à l' Imprimerie Nouvelle MICI EMBACI en Côte d' Ivoire. content: La startup tunisienne Rybsen, spécialisée dans le recyclage des eaux industrielles, a franchi une étape importante avec l' installation de sa première machine AquaClean en Afrique. L' équipement a été déployé au sein de l' Imprimerie Nouvelle MICI EMBACI, la plus grande imprimerie de Côte d' Ivoire, marquant ainsi le début de son expansion sur le continent. Le Centre de promotion des exportations (Cepex) a activement soutenu ce projet, en garantissant une visibilité médiatique à AquaClean et en facilitant les connexions avec des acteurs économiques locaux ainsi qu' avec l' ambassade de Tunisie en Côte d' Ivoire. Le Cepex a également pris en charge plusieurs frais, dont les billets d' avion, la prospection et l' hébergement. L' objectif de Rybsen est désormais d' attirer un plus grand nombre d' entreprises en Côte d' Ivoire et de mettre en place un bureau sur place, ce qui faciliterait considérablement le développement de l' entreprise. La startup souhaite ainsi mettre en avant son potentiel à l' international, notamment à travers l' exportation de ses technologies. Il est important de noter qu' AquaClean est une technologie brevetée permettant de recycler et de valoriser les eaux usées industrielles. Elle a été conçue pour répondre aux besoins croissants en gestion durable des ressources hydriques dans des secteurs à fort impact environnemental, tels que l' imprimerie, l' agroalimentaire et le textile, particulérement en Afrique. Rybsen ambitionne d' étendre son activité en Afrique subsaharienne, en ciblant de nouveaux secteurs industriels. L' entreprise mise sur son partenariat avec le Cepex pour renforcer sa présence sur le continent et accompagner les entreprises africaines dans leur transition écologique. Avec cette première implantation en Côte d' Ivoire, la startup tunisienne se positionne comme un acteur clé de l' innovation environnementale. D' autres installations sont prévues en Afrique de l' Ouest dans les mois à venir, consolidant ainsi son rôle de pionnier dans le domaine du recyclage des eaux industrielles. date: 2025-12-02 date_precision: day category: startup entities.startups[0].name: Rybsen entities.startups[0].country: Tunisia entities.startups[0].sector: Water Recycling source_url: origin_file: articles.json

metadata.name: La startup tunisienne Rybsen, spécialisée dans le recyclage des eaux industrielles, a franchi une étape importante avec l' installation de sa première machine AquaClean en Afrique

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: be47f31478e3 title: La startup fintech égyptienne Raseedi a acquis Kashat dans le cadre d' un accord en actions pour élargir ses services et introduire des prêts instantanés summary: Raseedi acquires Kashat to expand services and introduce instant loans content: La startup fintech égyptienne Raseedi a acquis Kashat dans le cadre d' un accord en actions pour élargir ses services et introduire des prêts instantanés. Fondée en 2018, Raseedi propose une application permettant aux utilisateurs sous-bancarisés d' effectuer des appels moins chers, de recevoir des conseils d' épargne, d' effectuer des paiements et d' accéder à des crédits instantanés sans historique de crédit. Kashat, également fondée en 2018, offre des microcrédits aux personnes exclues du système financier. Grâce à cet accord, les utilisateurs de Raseedi pourront bénéficier de prêts en espèces instantanés via Pharos Microfinance S.A.E., une filiale de Kashat. Les deux entreprises visent à étendre leur solution numérique à l' Afrique et à l' Asie pour mieux servir les populations sous-bancarisées. Ce partenariat fait de Raseedi et Kashat le premier fournisseur en Égypte à proposer des nano-prêts à grande échelle, favorisant ainsi l' inclusion financière dans la région. date: 2025-01-17 date_precision: day category: acquisition entities.startups[0].name: Raseedi entities.startups[0].country: Egypt entities.startups[0].sector: Fintech entities.startups[1].name: Kashat entities.startups[1].country: Egypt entities.startups[1].sector: Fintech source_url: origin_file: articles.json

metadata.name: La startup fintech égyptienne Raseedi a acquis Kashat dans le cadre d' un accord en actions pour élargir ses services et introduire des prêts instantanés

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: b4da236cd847 title: Le chef du gouvernement, Kamel Madouri, a reçu, mardi, au Palais du gouvernement à La Kasbah, Jihed Hannachi et Rabii Saidi, deux jeunes entrepreneurs spécialisés dans le développement des technologies souveraines liées aux données et à l' intelligence artificielle. summary: Le chef du gouvernement, Kamel Madouri, a reçu, mardi, au Palais du gouvernement à La Kasbah, Jihed Hannachi et Rabii Saidi, deux jeunes entrepreneurs spécialisés dans le développement des technologies souveraines liées aux données et à l' intelligence artificielle. content: Le chef du gouvernement, Kamel Madouri, a reçu, mardi, au Palais du gouvernement à La Kasbah, Jihed Hannachi et Rabii Saidi, deux jeunes entrepreneurs spécialisés dans le développement des technologies souveraines liées aux données et à l' intelligence artificielle. Ces derniers ont présenté leur projet, inspirés par les décisions issues du conseil ministériel du 30 novembre 2024, dédié à la simplification et à la digitalisation des procédures administratives. Ce conseil avait invité les startups tunisiennes à soumettre des propositions innovantes pour la création de services numériques au profit de l' administration. Selon un communiqué de la présidence du gouvernement, Kamel Madouri a salué l' initiative de ces jeunes entrepreneurs, mettant en avant leur adhésion volontaire à cette démarché, en phase avec la vision du président de la République, qui valorise la créativité et l' innovation des jeunes Tunisiens. Le chef du gouvernement a également souligné la qualité et le potentiel prometteur de leur projet, tant au niveau national qu' international, réaffirmant le soutien total du gouvernement à ces initiatives qui renforcent le principe d' autonomie et enrichissent le paysage économique et institutionnel. Madouri a profité de cette rencontre pour remercier les deux entrepreneurs pour leur engagement à utiliser leurs compétences dans le domaine des technologies souveraines afin de contribuer à la digitalisation de l' administration tunisienne. Les deux jeunes startupeurs ont également partagé leur parcours académique et professionnel, effectué à l' université de Tunis, avant de fonder leur entreprise au pôle technologique de Jendouba. Bien que leur startup ait rencontré un franc succès à l' international, ils ont choisi de revenir en Tunisie pour accélérer le développement des services et les rapprocher des citoyens. Lors de la réunion, ils ont présenté plusieurs propositions concrètes, notamment une cartographie des programmes, bases de données et logiciels permettant un suivi en temps réel des projets, aussi bien au niveau central que régional. date: 2025-01-15 date_precision: day category: startup ecosystem entities.startups[0].name: Startup de Jihed Hannachi et Rabii Saidi entities.startups[0].country: Tunisia source_url: origin_file: articles.json

metadata.name: Le chef du gouvernement, Kamel Madouri, a reçu, mardi, au Palais du gouvernement à La Kasbah, Jihed Hannachi et Rabii Saidi, deux jeunes entrepreneurs spécialisés dans le développement des technologies souveraines liées aux données et à l' intelligence artificielle.

Additional information: id: a6c5ea954347 title: La startup égyptienne ReNile, spécialisée dans l' agritech, a levé 450 000 dollars américains pour consolider ses activités sur le marché local et lancer ses opérations en Arabie saoudite summary: La startup égyptienne ReNile a levé 450 000 dollars américains pour consolider ses activités sur le marché local et lancer ses opérations en Arabie saoudite. content: La startup égyptienne ReNile, spécialisée dans l' agritech, a levé 450 000 dollars américains pour consolider ses activités sur le marché local et lancer ses opérations en Arabie saoudite. Créé en 2017, ReNile développe des solutions d' agriculture intelligente visant à améliorer la productivité agricole grâce à la technologie. Elle propose une gamme complète de produits pour surveiller et optimiser la qualité de l' eau, du sol et de l' air. Ses systèmes IoT permettent aux agriculteurs de suivre les conditions, d' automatiser les processus et d' augmenter leur efficacité. Cette nouvelle levée de fonds, obtenue auprès d' investisseurs non révélés, permettra à ReNile d' élargir ses activités et de s' implanter également au Koweït. date: 2025-10-01 date_precision: day category: startup entities.startups[0].name: ReNile entities.startups[0].country: Egypt entities.startups[0].sector: Agritech funding.amount: 450000 funding.currency: USD source_url: origin_file: articles.json

Additional information: id: 928888aa33ca title: Au cours de l'année 2024, le nombre des startups labelisées a dépassé le cap des 1 000, une réalisation importante pour ce projet summary: Le nombre des startups labelisées a dépassé le cap des 1 000 en 2024, selon les statistiques présentées. content: Au cours de l'année 2024, le nombre des startups labelisées a dépassé le cap des 1 000, une réalisation importante pour ce projet. Selon les statistiques présentées, 1 999 startups ont présenté leurs candidatures. Actuellement, il y a 938 labels actifs. En même temps, 441 pré-labels ont été attribués, dont 371 sont passés au stade du label. En même temps, il y a eu également 62 retraits de labels. Les chiffres, collectés par Entrepreneurs of Tunisia, montrent que 35,8% des startups sont fondées par des femmes et 79% ont été lancées par plus d'un fondateur. Des entités ont pu dépasser les frontières, et nous avons 36 startups qui opérent en France, 7 en Grande-Bretagne, 4 en Allemagne et aux États-Unis, 3 à Dubaï, 2 à Montréal, Belgique, Suisse, Russie et en Chine. Il y a également des startups qui ont pu s'installer aux Pays-Bas, au Maroc, Ontario, Turquie et en Corée du Sud. Par secteur, le premier est le Software, Big Data & Analysis avec 164 startups. Viennent ensuite le E-commerce & Marketplace (138) et Média, Gaming & Othercreative content (82). Par régions, le Grand-Tunis arrive premier avec 689 startups, dont 478 à Tunis et 126 à l'Ariana. Nous trouvons également Sousse avec 1126 entités et Sfax avec 54. Nabeul compte 41 startups et Bizerte 18. Monastir arrive ensuite avec 15 jeunes pépites, Médenine également (dont 3 à Djerba). Paradoxalement, 14 gouvernorats abritent moins de 10 startups chacune, ce qui confirme la réalité sur laquelle nous avons toujours insisté: il y a peu de dynamique économique à l'intérieur du pays. Tant qu'il n'y a pas de tissu industriel avec lequel il est possible de coopérer, il est difficile de s'imposer en tant que jeune entreprise. Nous avons un exemple choquant, celui de Siliana, où il n'y a aucune startup! La zone ouest du pays ne compte que 45 startups, ce qui est très peu, alors que nous sommes certains qu'un grand nombre de promoteurs de startups dans les villes à la côte provient de ces zones. In fine, quelle que soit la taille de la société, les défis sont les même. Sans vraies solutions, le gap entre les régions va s'aggraver, ancrant les différences socio-économiques qui secouent le pays. date: 2025-09-01 date_precision: day category: startup ecosystem source_url: origin_file: articles.json

metadata.name: Au cours de l'année 2024, le nombre des startups labelisées a dépassé le cap des 1 000, une réalisation importante pour ce projet

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: c70944f37f55 title: L' écosystème africain des startups a enregistré des changements notables dans la répartition des financements entre ses régions en 2024 summary: L' écosystème africain des startups a enregistré des changements notables dans la répartition des financements entre ses régions en 2024. content: L' écosystème africain des startups a enregistré des changements notables dans la répartition des financements entre ses régions en 2024. Toutefois, le Kenya, le Nigeria, l' Égypte et l' Afrique du Sud continuent à dominer. Le rapport Africa The Big Deal a révélé que ces quatre pays ont accaparé 84% de tous les financements de startups sur le continent. En tout, les startups africaines ont levé 2,2 milliards de dollars, -25% en glissement annuel. Par région, l' Afrique de l' Est a attiré la plus grande partie des capitaux, et ce, pour la deuxième année consécutive. Ses startups ont pu mobiliser 725 millions de dollars, en repli de 18% par rapport à 2023. Le Kenya a été le fer de lance de la région, attirant 638 millions de dollars, avec 29% de tous les fonds levés sur le continent. La Tanzanie arrive derrière dans la région, avec 53 millions de dollars, se classant ainsi au septième rang sur le continent, suivie de l' Ouganda avec 19 millions de dollars. L' Afrique de l' Ouest a enregistré des opérations de 587 millions de dollars de financement. Le Nigeria a attiré plus de 400 millions de dollars. Parmi les autres pays performants, il y a le Ghana (68 millions de dollars), le Bénin (50 millions), la Côte d' Ivoire (33 millions) et le Sénégal (22 millions). Côté baisse, il y a l' Afrique du Sud qui a vu les flux financiers vers ses startups reculer de 34%, à 395 millions de dollars. Idem pour l' Afrique centrale qui n' a pu lever que 5 millions de dollars en 2024, soit plus de dix fois moins que son total en 2023. Enfin, l' Afrique du Nord a mobilisé 478 millions de dollars l' année dernière, soit 22% du financement total du continent. La baisse a été également sensible avec un repli de 35% par rapport à 2023. L' Égypte a contribué à 84% du total de la région avec 402 millions de dollars. Le Maroc, en revanche, a fait preuve de résilience, levant 70 millions de dollars et se classant cinquième au classement général. L' année n' était pas exceptionnellement bonne pour la Tunisie, où une bonne partie des startups se cherchent encore. date: 2025-08-01 date_precision: day category: startup ecosystem funding.amount: 2200000000 funding.currency: USD source_url: origin_file: articles.json

metadata.name: L' écosystème africain des startups a enregistré des changements notables dans la répartition des financements entre ses régions en 2024

Additional information: id: 49c0291c5714 title: Kenyan social commerce startup Twiva raises funding from Jobtech Alliance summary: Kenyan startup Twiva, which transforms the opportunity landscape for African creators by replacing sporadic brand deals with structured demand, performance-based earnings, and integrated financial services, has raised an undisclosed amount of funding from the Jobtech Alliance. content: Kenyan startup Twiva, which is transforming the opportunity landscape for African creators by replacing sporadic brand deals with structured demand, performance-based earnings, and integrated financial services, has raised an undisclosed amount of funding from the Jobtech Alliance. Disrupt Africa reported in October 2022 on the launch of the Jobtech Alliance, an ecosystem-building initiative around inclusive jobtech in Africa steered by Mercy Corps and BFA Global, which aims to create an enabling environment for entrepreneurs to build platforms that deliver quality livelihoods, are inclusive, and enable users to engage in decent work. It has run several cohorts, but is now also making direct investments in relevant ventures, most recently in Nigeria's Bumpa and Kenya's Flowcart, both e-commerce ventures. Jobtech Alliance has now backed Kenyan startup Twiva, which is tackling the"dual crisis" of youth unemployment and MSME barriers with its influencer-powered social commerce platform, which enables MSMEs to market and sell their products and services through online gig workers. The investment in Twiva, which took part in the fourth edition of the Visa Africa Fintech Accelerator last year, is undisclosed, but Jobtech Alliance has already been working with the startup for more than six months, providing bespoke venture support and engaging with creators on the platform."In the process, we confirmed our hypothesis that building the infrastructure to generate consistent, repeat brand demand is the (rewarding!) challenge that platforms need to tackle," Jobtech Alliance said. Explaining the rationale behind the investment, Jobtech said it was clear that creator audiences are growing in Africa, with platforms multiplying and monetisation tools expanding."But reliable incomes remain an issue. Even among top-tier creators, income stability is far from guaranteed. Earnings frequently fluctuate due to seasonal brand campaigns, platform algorithm changes, and shifts in audience engagement," it said. In addressing this, Twiva stood out to the organisation for two main reasons."It deeply understands demand, and it deeply understands creators. The platform demonstrates that when you structure demand, embed payments, build skills, and treat creators as distributed businesses, you can build repeat revenue and customers for brands and more durable income for creators," Jobtech Alliance said. The majority of influencer marketplaces in Africa tend to have a singular focus on discovery. They aggregate talent and hope that demand follows. Twiva does the opposite by structuring deman

metadata.name: Kenyan social commerce startup Twiva raises funding from Jobtech Alliance

Additional information: id: f7afd5cc5beb title: Nigeria's Siiqo launches social commerce infrastructure on Telegram summary: Nigerian startup Siiqo launches full commerce infrastructure on Telegram, enabling SMEs to list products, take orders, collect payments via fiat or crypto, and manage operations with an escrow-based trust layer directly within the chat platform. content: Nigerian startup Siiqo launches commerce infrastructure on Telegram, helping users increase their sales and better manage their businesses. Founded by Okerere Innocent and Linda Kolapa Ogochukwu, Siiqo aims to help users build brands by creating online storefronts and portfolios, and getting discovered by a wider audience. It also allows them to get paid, via fiat or crypto, and manage business operations efficiently and effectively. The startup has now launched a full commerce infrastructure on Telegram to deepen social commerce in Nigeria."We didn't set out to build on Telegram. Like most platforms in this space, our roadmap assumed the obvious next step after the web was a native mobile app. But the more time we spent with the SMEs and vendors actually using Siiqo, the clearer one pattern became - they weren't waiting for an app," said Innocent."They were already running entire customer relationships - orders, prices, follow-ups, disputes - inside Telegram and WhatsApp threads, manually, with no system behind any of it." That observation is what pushed Siiqo to build a full commerce bot directly inside Telegram, instead of doubling down on app development. Vendors can now list products, take orders, and collect payment without ever leaving the chat interface their customers already trust."Critically, we built our escrow-based trust layer directly into that experience, so the same protection against fake payment alerts and scam transactions that defines Siiqo on the web now travels with vendors into Telegram," Innocent said."We think this is a genuinely interesting strategic bet, not just a feature release: that the next phase of Nigerian social commerce isn't going to be won by better apps, but by infrastructure - trust, payments, verification - that lives natively inside the chat platforms where Nigerian SMEs are already operating." date: 2026-06-30 date_precision: day region: Africa category: startup source_url: origin_file: newsAll.json

metadata.name: Nigeria's Siiqo launches social commerce infrastructure on Telegram

Additional information: id: c26f0a941a54 title: How Nigeria's Viewlocked uses AI to power travel planning platform summary: Nigerian startup Viewlocked, founded in 2025 by Abbey Ola, is an AI-powered travel planning platform that unifies trip discovery, organization, and booking in one place. The bootstrapped company connects travelers with local travel businesses across accommodations, experiences, transportation, and more, operating on a commission-based model. Viewlocked has onboarded partners in over 15 countries globally and is currently exploring a seed funding round for product development and expansion. content: Nigerian startup Viewlocked is an AI-powered travel planning platform that helps travellers discover, organise, and eventually book every part of a trip in one place. Founded in 2025 by Abbey Ola, Viewlocked provides travel businesses with a platform to showcase their services to travellers who are actively planning trips, and helps travellers plan and pay for their trips."Today, planning a trip often requires jumping between multiple websites and apps to find accommodations, experiences, transportation, guides, rentals, and local services. Viewlocked brings these elements together into a single platform, allowing travellers to plan more efficiently while discovering trusted local businesses and unique experiences," Ola said. The idea emerged from observing how fragmented travel planning remains despite advances in travel technology. Viewlocked aims to unify those experiences into a single planning environment while helping travellers discover local businesses that may otherwise be difficult to find. The bootstrapped Viewlocked, which recently began conversations around a seed funding round to support product development, market expansion, and growth initiatives, has built a global network of travel partners across multiple destinations before launch, entirely through founder-led outreach and without paid marketing. Viewlocked currently features partners across Morocco, Indonesia, Spain, Egypt, Sri Lanka, the Philippines, Norway, Colombia, Croatia, Tanzania, Thailand, Zimbabwe, France, the Maldives, and Mexico. The startup operates on a commission-based model where partners can join and list services for free, while Viewlocked earns a commission on successful bookings generated through the platform. date: 2026-06-29 date_precision: day region: Africa category: startup source_url: origin_file: newsAll.json

metadata.name: How Nigeria's Viewlocked uses AI to power travel planning platform

Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 3efd136f1a86 title: Village Capital distributes $500k grants to 32 climate startups summary: Village Capital concluded its three-year Empowering Sustainable Entrepreneurship Africa (ESEA) initiative, deploying $500,000 in grant funding to 32 climate startups across Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, and Tanzania. The grants targeted climate adaptation, food security, blue economy, and renewable energy ventures. content: Village Capital has announced the conclusion of the Empowering Sustainable Entrepreneurship Africa (ESEA), a three-year initiative established to strengthen locally-led entrepreneurial ecosystems and expand pathways to capital for climate startups across Africa. Village Capital has partnered Norad - the Norwegian Agency for Development Cooperation to run ESEA, which was designed around a core belief that entrepreneurs do not scale in isolation. Through ESEA, Village Capital worked with 14 locally-led ecosystem builders across Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, and Tanzania to strengthen their organisations and test a co-delivery model for founder support. Building on this foundation, it partnered with five ecosystem builders in 2025 to co-deliver investment-readiness programmes for 150 climate startups across the five markets. As the initiative concludes, Village Capital has deployed US$500,000 in grant funding to 32 startups addressing critical sustainability challenges across the continent. The largest share of funding was directed toward climate adaptation startups, which received US$225,000. Food security startups received US$125,000, blue economy startups received US$100,000, and renewable energy startups received US$50,000."At Village Capital, we' ve learned that the challenge isn' t a lack of entrepreneurial talent. Too often, the challenge is that the systems around entrepreneurs are not designed to help promising startups access the networks, resources, and capital they need to grow," said Nakami Walunywa, regional director for Africa at Village Capital."ESEA gave us an opportunity to test what happens when you work through local organisations that are deeply embedded in their markets. We saw how local knowledge, trusted founder relationships, and ecosystem intelligence can help surface stronger pipelines and create more effective pathways between entrepreneurs and capital. Those lessons are shaping how we think about ecosystem building, not as a parallel effort to investing, but as essential infrastructure for directing capital where it can create the greatest long-term value." date: 2026-06-29 date_precision: day region: Africa category: funding source_url: origin_file: newsAll.json

metadata.name: Village Capital distributes $500k grants to 32 climate startups

Additional information: id: da950d78f421 title: Jobtech Alliance launches startup accelerator programmes to improve livelihoods in Africa summary: The Jobtech Alliance, a partnership between Mercy Corps and BFA Global, has launched two programmes - the Catalyst Jobtech Accelerator and the Jobtech Innovation Lab - to support early-stage digital platforms connecting people to work opportunities across Africa. The accelerator will host two cohorts of four innovators each, targeting startups operating in Kenya, Uganda, Nigeria, Senegal, Ethiopia, Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Sudan, and South Sudan. Selected companies receive capital, venture-building support, and access to industry experts and mentors. content: The Jobtech Alliance, an ecosystem-building initiative around inclusive jobtech in Africa steered by Mercy Corps and BFA Global, has launched two programmes - the Catalyst Jobtech Accelerator and the Jobtech Innovation Lab - designed to support digital platforms that connect people to work opportunities that build livelihoods. A partnership between Mercy Corps and BFA Global, the Jobtech Alliance aims to create an enabling environment for entrepreneurs to build platforms that deliver quality livelihoods, are inclusive, and enable users to engage in decent work. Building on the experience of BFA Global' s Catalyst Fund model, the Catalyst Job Accelerator will host two cohorts of four early-stage innovators creating affordable, accessible, and appropriate jobtech solutions that connect young people to jobs. The Catalyst Job Accelerator will focus on innovations with the potential to scale and meaningfully increase jobs and income, and grant resources that would substantially impact the team' s ability to test and improve their product. The target market or operations of the startup should be in Kenya, Uganda, Nigeria, Senegal, Ethiopia, Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Sudan, or South Sudan."By building an ecosystem where entrepreneurs have access to knowledge and networks that can help scale their businesses, we' re starting to change the conversation about the future of work and what can be achieved," said Michelle Hassan, Kenya country manager at BFA Global. The Jobtech Innovation Lab partners with jobtech platforms on individual sprints which have the potential to enhance scale, impact, or inclusivity of platforms with their users. This could include developing new products or services; enhancing products, operations, or marketing in order to better deliver services, reach new markets, or remove barriers to access for marginalised populations; and supporting market expansion, particularly towards underserved markets."There are many great opportunities for jobtech platforms to increase their scale, impact, and inclusivity on the continent, but there is a lack of time, capital, and capacity to deliver on them. We' re keen to work with these platforms to further their business goals and improve outcomes for their platform users," said Chris Maclay, Mercy Corps Jobtech Allianc

metadata.name: Jobtech Alliance launches startup accelerator programmes to improve livelihoods in Africa

Additional information: id: 760a42921062 title: SA circular fashion startup Kloset Klub raises seed funding summary: South African circular fashion platform Kloset Klub has raised an undisclosed seed funding round from Thinkroom and co-investor Frank Smit to expand operations. content: South Africa' s Kloset Klub, a fast-growing circular fashion platform, has secured a round of seed funding to help it expand operations. Founded by Phumi Körber, Kloset Klub helps people buy and sell quality pre-loved clothing through curated resale, peer-to-peer shopping, and managed closet services. The platform helps sellers unlock value from their wardrobes while giving buyers access to premium pre-loved fashion at more accessible prices. The startup has now raised an undisclosed amount of seed funding from Thinkroom, an African entrepreneurship and innovation firm, and co-investor Frank Smit."This investment is more than just capital, it' s a vote of confidence in the journey we' ve been on," said Körber."Building a business is never linear, and having partners like Thinkroom and Frank, who understand that reality, makes a huge difference. We' re excited to keep building something that not only looks good, but does good too.""We' ve watched Phumi build Kloset Klub through real challenges, real pivots, requiring real grit," said Catherine Young, founder of Thinkroom."Phumi' s resilience and her approach to building a business stood out early on. We are incredibly proud to be writing the first cheque into Kloset Klub and to support Phumi as she scales." date: 2026-08-06 date_precision: day region: Africa category: funding source_url: origin_file: newsAll.json

metadata.name: SA circular fashion startup Kloset Klub raises seed funding

Additional information: id: fed549ac0805 title: Winners of Nedbank N*ovation Data and Analytics Masters Challenge announced summary: Nedbank announced winners of its 2026 Data and Analytics Masters Challenge in Johannesburg, with prize money of ZAR500,000 and fast-tracked recruitment for top performers. content: South Africa' s Nedbank has announced the winners of the Nedbank N*ovation Data and Analytics Masters Challenge 2026, following weeks of rigorous virtual competition and an in-person finale in Johannesburg. The national Data and Analytics Challenge is designed to identify and recruit South Africa' s most advanced data and analytics practitioners, and this year' s edition culminated in a live contest where finalists were tested on their ability to solve complex, real-world banking problems. The winner of the data engineering track was Mogale Thobejane, with Tshifhiwa Gift Moila and Tinashe Chinyati coming in second and third place. The data science and machine learning track was won by Edward Rycroft, with Alex Jacobs in second and Vusani Khosa in third. Contestants shared ZAR500,000 (US$30,000) in prize money. The top performers will benefit from a fast-tracked recruitment process, highlighting the bank' s ongoing investment in building future-ready data and analytics capabilities. In collaboration with partners including OfferZen, Zindi, Otinga, and Microsoft, the challenge potentially connects top talent directly with career opportunities."This challenge is about more than competition, it reflects our belief that data excellence is a strategic capability, and this challenge was a novel one with a positive outcome demonstrated by the level of quality on display at the challenge," said Nedbank Group technology executive Thando Lukhele. date: 2026-05-20 date_precision: day region: Africa category: event source_url: origin_file: newsAll.json

metadata.name: Winners of Nedbank N*ovation Data and Analytics Masters Challenge announced

text:

Additional information: id: f72189a8fb27 title: Kenya's Fikra launches AI inference API for African developers, startups, businesses summary: Kenyan startup Fikra API launches an OpenAI-compatible AI inference API tailored for African developers with M-Pesa payments, local currency pricing, and flat-rate token pricing of $1 per 2 million tokens. content: Kenya's Fikra API has launched an AI inference API built specifically for African developers, startups, and businesses. Founded by James Miano earlier this year, Fikra API is an OpenAI-compatible inference API targeting African developers, with M-Pesa payments built in and pricing in local terms rather than USD. Developers can send requests to Fikra's endpoint and get responses from large language models, the same way they would with OpenAI or Anthropic, but with pricing, payment methods, and token economics designed for African markets."If you are a developer or startup founder in Nairobi, Lagos, or Cape Town and you want to build an AI-powered product, the standard path is OpenAI or Anthropic. Both require a minimum spend of around US$20 before you can access the API," Miano told Disrupt Africa."That figure sounds small, but in Nairobi US$20 is close to a week's salary for many people. For those who can afford it, the problem does not stop there, a frontier model output pricing is expensive and burns through tokens quickly, forcing constant top-ups that compound the cost." The second barrier is payment."Most people in Africa are not cardholders. OpenAI and Anthropic require a credit or debit card. Mobile money is how the majority of people here actually transact, and none of the major AI API providers support it," Miano said. Fikra API exists to solve both. Fully-bootstrapped, the startup onboarded its first 10 users within days of launch."We have also shipped two proprietary models - a custom embeddings model ready for inference, and Fikra Nano 1B which is a ternary weight fine-tuned model we built in-house. The ternary weight pipeline is a genuinely uncommon capability and gives us a technical foundation most API providers at this stage do not have," said Miano. Currently only active in Kenya, with payment infrastructure live via Paystack which covers much of sub-Saharan Africa, Fikra's model is built to scale continent-wide."Any African developer with a smartphone and mobile money access is the target user," said Miano. The startup charges US$1 per two million tokens."We do not split input and output pricing the way most providers do, it is one flat rate. Payment is via Paystack, which supports M-Pesa and mobile money across multiple African markets. We are pre-revenue at this stage with the commercial push beginning at launch," said Miano."The main challenge is onboarding friction, getting a developer from signup to their first successful API call as fast as possible, because if that moment takes too long you lose them. We identified this early and have been iterating on it. The broader difficulty is distribution.

metadata.name: Kenya's Fikra launches AI inference API for African developers, startups, businesses

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 195ee7628c51 title: Holocene closes Southern Africa's first dedicated climate-tech fund summary: VC firm Holocene announced the final close of Holocene Ventures Fund I (HVF1), Southern Africa's first dedicated high-growth climate tech fund. The fund has backed 10 companies in 18 months, creating over 500 jobs, achieving a 2x markup on invested capital, and attracting $8 in follow-on capital for every $1 invested. Portfolio companies include FARO, ScootHero, and Yongeza, each receiving at least 12 months of hands-on operational support. content: VC firm Holocene has announced the final close of Holocene Ventures Fund I (HVF1), Southern Africa's first dedicated high-growth climate tech fund. Holocene targets promising local founders at the earliest stages who lack capital and hands-on operational support to scale, especially in a nascent sector such as climate-tech. Through HVF1, it has backed 10 companies in the 18 months it has been deployed, creating over 500 jobs and achieving a 2x markup on invested capital, while attracting US$8 in follow-on capital for every US$1 it invested."It's clear we need to dramatically accelerate the pace of climate innovation in Africa," founder Josh Romisher said."The continent will double in size, urbanise, and begin to truly consume during our lifetimes. That can be seen as an impending climate catastrophe or a massive innovation opportunity. We choose to view it as the latter." Portfolio ventures include FARO, a circular economy company built around technology-enabled supply chains for unused inventory; ScootHero, which is deploying over 500 electric motorbikes and more than 50 battery-swap stations across South Africa; and Yongeza, an e-mobility infrastructure company in Uganda. Each investment comes with at least 12 months of embedded, high-touch support from a team of globally trained specialists: operators, executives-in-residence, and experts in revenue growth, fundraising, and grant writing."What excites me most is not just that Holocene backed us early, it's that they got in the trenches with us," said Wahlied Cole, founder and CEO of ScootHero."When we needed help with go-to-market, they brought in operators. When we were hitting a ceiling on capital, they helped us unlock grants and asset finance. It wasn't just capital. It was a partnership built on real understanding of what African founders need." Jonathan Smit, exited founder and HVF1 investor, said he invested in Holocene because he believes climate innovation will be one of the defining challenges and opportunities of our generation, particularly in Africa."What stood out to me was not just the investment thesis, but the quality of the team and the hands-on way they support founders. Holocene is helping build the ecosystem and companies that can deliver meaningful climate impact while creating lasting economic value," he said. The final close marks the beginning of the fund's value-creation phase - the work of driving portfolio companies towar

metadata.name: Holocene closes Southern Africa's first dedicated climate-tech fund

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 3777ba7432f5 title: How Nigeria's Sokofa connects diaspora buyers with African artisans, small vendors summary: Nigerian startup Sokofa operates a marketplace linking diaspora buyers in Europe and North America with verified African artisans and small vendors across fashion, art, and beauty. The platform offers made-to-measure ordering with up to 16 custom measurements, an AI virtual try-on tool generating previews in under 25 seconds, and vendor verification. Founded in late 2025, Sokofa is self-funded and aims to put African artisans on the global e-commerce map on their own terms. content: Nigerian startup Sokofa is a marketplace connecting diaspora buyers across Europe and North America with verified African artisans and small vendors across fashion, art, and beauty. Founded late last year, Sokofa provides access to fashion listings that let a buyer enter their own measurements directly on the product page, up to 16 specific fields depending on the garment, and have the piece cut by hand by the actual vendor, not a factory running standard sizes. The same listings carry an AI virtual try-on tool, where a buyer uploads a photo and gets a generated preview of the outfit on them in under 25 seconds, with the photo deleted after 24 hours."The vendors are independent artisans currently based in Africa, selling Ankara sets, Adire chords, kaftans, and traditional native wear, verified by Sokofa before they are allowed to list. It is a small, real example of an African startup solving the fit and trust problem that diaspora shoppers have dealt with for years, using a mix of straightforward custom order infrastructure and AI rather than either alone," founder Tope Abuloye told Disrupt Africa. At its core, Abuloye said Sokofa is about putting Africa on the map in global e-commerce"on its own terms"."Diaspora buyers wanting authentic African fashion, art, and beauty have had two weak options: generic marketplaces with no fit guarantee or vendor verification, or relying on word of mouth tailors back home with long lead times and no buyer protection," said Abuloye."We wanted African artisans to be discoverable and trusted by a global audience the same way any other category of maker is, not treated as a niche or an afterthought. Sokofa brings vendor verification, made to measure ordering, and AI fit preview into one checkout flow, a combination we haven' t seen elsewhere in this category." Sokofa is self-funded, built on personal savings and support from family and friends, and has already listed multiple vendors, and made a number of sales. Vendors are based across Africa, while buyers are across Europe and North America."We' re actively expanding vendor recruitment to include Africans in the diaspora who want to sell African-inspired products themselves, several have already expressed interest in listing on the platform even while based outside the continent," Abuloye said. Sokofa runs on a commission model, taking a percentage of each sale. date: 2

metadata.name: How Nigeria's Sokofa connects diaspora buyers with African artisans, small vendors

Additional information: id: 7ffb2c2d7f8d title: Catalyst Fund' s $30m 2nd close deepens bet on Africa's climate resilience founders summary: Pan-African venture fund Catalyst Fund announced a $30 million second close, bringing total capital closer to its target. The fund backs pre-seed to Series A startups building climate resilience solutions for underserved communities. New investors include IFC, FASA, Shell Foundation, Trafigura Foundation, Speedinvest, BlinkCV, and high-net-worth individuals, joining earlier backers FSD Africa and Cisco Foundation. Catalyst Fund expects final close later in 2026. content: Catalyst Fund, the pan-African venture fund and venture builder backing founders from pre-seed to Series A, has announced a US$30 million second close, bringing it closer to its target and adding fresh momentum to its mission of strengthening climate resilience across the continent. Catalyst Fund is a pre-seed VC fund and accelerator backing high-impact tech startups that seek to improve the resilience of underserved, climate-vulnerable communities. It partners with mission-driven founders that share our vision of a world where every individual has the tools and opportunities they need to thrive. Until 2023, the organisation offered grant capital to selected startups, but it has since switched to an equity-based model. Its first close of that fund was in September 2023, and the second close brings together IFC, FASA, Shell Foundation, Trafigura Foundation, Speedinvest, BlinkCV, and a group of high-net-worth individuals, joining early backers FSD Africa, Cisco Foundation, and others who validated the thesis at first close. Catalyst Fund expects to reach final close later this year."Climate adaptation is one of the defining investment themes of the next decade, especially in Africa, where the need is immediate, and the entrepreneurial talent is extraordinary. This second close allows us to double down on our mission: backing ambitious founders building practical, scalable solutions for a climate-changed world, and supporting them not just with capital, but with the hands-on venture-building support they need to grow," said Maelis Carraro, founder and general partner at Catalyst Fund. date: 2026-03-07 date_precision: day region: Africa category: funding source_url: origin_file: newsAll.json

metadata.name: Catalyst Fund' s $30m 2nd close deepens bet on Africa's climate resilience founders

Entity type: article
Additional information: id: ca11ccb001b3 title: African Payment Providers Bypass SWIFT for Stablecoin Settlement summary: African payment service providers (PSPs) are increasingly adopting stablecoin settlement via USDC and USDT to bypass SWIFT's slow, costly correspondent banking system. Swiss-based institutional OTC desk FinchTrade facilitates this shift, offering same-day settlement, reduced FX exposure, and predictable fees. The article highlights a $1M Nigeria-to-Germany payment costing $5,000-$8,000 via stablecoins versus $40,000-$70,000 via SWIFT, with settlement compressed from days to hours. Regulatory frameworks in Nigeria and South Africa are evolving to support this infrastructure, with expansion expected to more African corridors. content: For African PSPs and EMIs handling cross-border B2B settlement, correspondent banking has become a structural drag. SWIFT settlement windows run T+3 to T+5. Cumulative fees hit 3% to 7% across intermediary banks. Working capital sits tied up in days-long FX exposure. These are now line items PSPs measure against alternatives that didn't exist 18 months ago. FinchTrade, a Swiss-based institutional OTC desk, sees this shift daily. The company serves African payment companies as well as other international flows where stablecoin settlement through USDC and USDT has become an operational default."For our PSP clients, this is no longer about offering crypto products to end users," says Nicola Boldrini, growth lead at FinchTrade."It's about whether their cross-border settlement actually settles the same day or takes a week." Three operational realities are driving the migration: settlement reliability, FX exposure compression, and cost predictability. A Nigerian importer paying a German supplier $1 million faces compounding differences: SWIFT settlement typically costs $40,000 to $70,000 with four to five days of FX volatility, while the same payment routed through Naira-to-USDC-to-Euro clears within hours at $5,000 to $8,000 total cost. FinchTrade provides the crypto-fiat conversion layer and connects African PSPs to global stablecoin liquidity, using regulated stablecoin issuers, institutional custody (Fireblocks), and compliance platforms. FinchTrade holds a Swiss license through VQF and has processed over $1 billion in client trading volume since launch, running corridor settlement across Nigerian naira, Ghanaian cedi, Kenyan shilling, and South African rand. The business impact includes settlement time compressed from days to hours, all-in costs reduced by an order of magnitude, and working capital released from in-flight FX exposure. The shift also opens corridors that correspondent banking has historically underserved. Regulatory frameworks are maturing: the Central Bank of Nigeria has indicated openness to stablecoin payment infrastructure in B2B contexts, and South Africa's SARB framework provides workable guidelines. Additional corridors positioned to follow include Tanzania, Uganda, Senegal, and Côte d'Ivoire. Risks

metadata.name: African Payment Providers Bypass SWIFT for Stablecoin Settlement

Additional information: id: 77b0a33784b1 title: Egypt's BrainsMingle raises $400k seed funding summary: Egyptian AI-driven professional network BrainsMingle has secured $400,000 in seed funding from BasharSoft Group, Egypt's leading human capital technology group operating WUZZUF and Forasna. content: Egypt's BrainsMingle, an AI-driven, video-first professional network designed to prioritise authentic human connection, has raised US$400,000 in seed funding. Founded by Belal Amin and Yousef Gamal, BrainsMingle allows experts to deliver live sessions, build thriving communities, manage bookings, and earn from their expertise while ambitious professionals discover world-class knowledge, engage with experts across the globe, and connect with peers who will shape their careers. BrainsMingle, which has already attracted professionals and communities from more than 90 countries, has announced a US$400,000 strategic seed investment from BasharSoft Group, Egypt's leading human capital technology group, which operates WUZZUF and Forasna. This partnership represents BasharSoft's first strategic investment since its acquisition of iCareer last year. BasharSoft Group, which serves over 9 million users through its flagship platforms WUZZUF and Forasna, as well as iCareer and Recruitera, is betting on BrainsMingle to lead the next generation of professional engagement and knowledge-sharing technologies."We built BrainsMingle around one belief: the most valuable professional moments happen between people, not between people and content. The world does not need another feed to scroll or another profile to maintain. It needs a home, a single place where an ambitious professional can show up, share what they know, and build a community around it," Amin said."BrainsMingle was never built for one country or one region. It was built for every professional on the planet who has real expertise to share and no single place worthy of it. BasharSoft's investment is a powerful validation of that vision and a commitment to building it the right way." Ameer Sherif, executive chairman of BasharSoft, said his company has been connecting talent to opportunity for 15 years."We identified a significant gap in how knowledge and experience are shared online. BrainsMingle has built the technology necessary to fill this gap, replacing multiple global tools with a single, sophisticated network that resonates with our mission to empower 50 million people in their careers by 2030," he said. date: 2026-03-07 date_precision: day region: Africa category: funding source_url: origin_file: newsAll.json

Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 5475bd538afe title: Cascador invests over $5m in 7 Nigerian tech startups summary: Africa-focused platform Cascador deployed over $5 million across seven Nigerian startups at its second annual pitch day, including debt and equity investments in Agriarche, Koolboks, Powerstove, First Electric, Fortics, Stears, and Indigenius AI. content: Cascador, an Africa-focused platform for growth-stage founders building businesses with social impact, has deployed over US$5 million into seven innovative startups at its second annual pitch day. Disrupt Africa reported last March that Cascador had announced the launch of its Catalytic Fund, a US$2 million annual initiative providing tailored financial support to Cascador alumni companies. Since its founding in 2019, Cascador has supported over 60 entrepreneurs who have collectively raised more than US$55 million in capital. The company hosted its second annual on June 3, where over US$5 million of growth capital was deployed to seven innovative African entrepreneurs, who pitched their vision and growth strategies before an influential audience of business leaders and a distinguished panel of judges. The funding recipients included Agriarche, which secured US$1.7 million in debt funding; Koolboks, which banked US$1.4 million in debt; Powerstove, which secured debt funding worth US$1.2 million; and First Electric, which raised US$357,000 in debt. The other funded ventures were Fortics, which raised US$142,000 in debt; Stears, which banked US$450,000 in equity funding; and Indigenius AI, which raised US$250,000 in equity investment."In just two years, Pitch Day has awarded more than US$9 million to growth-stage African founders, helping to build a new generation of entrepreneurs equipped to scale transformative businesses. We' re now looking for the next cohort of exceptional founders to join our 2026 ScaleUp programme and hope to see them on stage at the next Pitch Day," said Dave DeLucia, founder of Cascador."Cascador' s ScaleUp programme built upon my team' s ability to translate learning into action by helping us refine our message and market position, adjust our funding strategy, and adapt without defensiveness. The Catalytic Fund due diligence team assessed Agriarche' s financial strength, resourcefulness, and track record of success, and they rewarded our high-potential for scale and impact today by awarding a new NGN2.5 billion credit facility to power our growth," said Deina Mayaki of Agriarche, the largest funding recipient from the 2026 Catalytic Fund. date: 2026-08-06 date_precision: day region: Africa category: funding source_url: origin_file: newsAll.json

metadata.name: Cascador invests over $5m in 7 Nigerian tech startups

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 8d2f45be9e7c title: Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira. They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive summary: The article argues that stablecoins like USDT and USDC are not undermining the Nigerian naira but are instead providing essential financial infrastructure for businesses and freelancers to manage currency volatility, preserve value, and facilitate international payments. It highlights Nigeria's $92.1 billion in crypto transactions in 2024, driven by utility, and mentions Monica.Cash as a platform bridging stablecoin-to-naira conversion. content: The conversation around stablecoins in Nigeria often starts from the wrong premise, writes Mbah Casmir, founder and CEO of Monica Cash, a cryptocurrency-to-naira exchange platform helping individuals and businesses across Nigeria convert digital assets seamlessly. For years, digital assets, particularly dollar-backed stablecoins such as USDT and USDC, have been framed as threats to the naira. Critics argue that their growing adoption encourages capital flight, weakens confidence in the local currency, and accelerates dollarisation. While those concerns may sound reasonable, they overlook what is actually happening across Nigeria' s economy. The reality is that most Nigerians are not turning to stablecoins because they want to speculate on cryptocurrency. They are turning to them because they are looking for practical ways to navigate economic uncertainty, preserve value, and keep businesses running. For thousands of small businesses, importers, exporters, freelancers, and remote workers, stablecoins have quietly become part of everyday financial planning. Consider the experience of a small importer sourcing products from suppliers abroad. A transaction that looks profitable on Monday can easily become a loss before payment is finally settled. For many businesses operating on tight margins, that kind of volatility makes it extremely difficult to plan with confidence. Every delay creates uncertainty, and every exchange rate movement can directly affect profitability. Stablecoins have become one of the tools helping businesses manage that risk. Rather than holding funds in a currency experiencing rapid fluctuations, many business owners temporarily preserve value in digital dollars until transactions are completed. This is not an attempt to replace the naira. It is a response to the realities of operating in a highly volatile financial environment. The same pattern is playing out among Nigerian freelancers and remote professionals. Nigeria has become one of Africa' s leading hubs for digital talent, with professionals increasingly working for clients across North America, Europe, and other international markets. Yet receiving and managing international payments through traditional channels can still involve delays, friction, and conversion challenges. Stablecoins solves this problem by providing a faster and more flexible alternative that allows many professionals to exercise greater control ove

metadata.name: Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira. They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive

Additional information: id: a4a7d0a16d4e title: Nigeria's StableKlin is a behavioural intelligence platform designed for cleaning businesses summary: Nigerian startup StableKlin, founded by Dehinde Sogbanmu, offers a behavioural intelligence platform that helps cleaning businesses detect early signs of customer disengagement by analyzing booking patterns and behavioural changes. The self-funded startup is currently focused on the UK cleaning market and seeks pilot users to refine its dashboard and insights. content: Nigerian startup StableKlin is a behavioural intelligence platform designed for cleaning businesses and other recurring service providers, which helps business owners understand something that is often difficult to see until it is too late - changes in customer behaviour. Most cleaning businesses already track bookings, customer details, invoices and payments."What they often do not have is a clear way to identify when a loyal customer is gradually becoming less engaged. Customers rarely announce that they are leaving. More often, they start booking less frequently, delay appointments, become less predictable, or quietly drift away over time," said Dehinde Sogbanmu, StableKlin' s founder. StableKlin is built to detect those early warning signs. The platform analyses patterns such as a customer' s usual booking frequency, the time since their last service, changes in scheduling habits, consistency over time, and other behavioural indicators. It then highlights customers whose behaviour is beginning to move away from what has historically been normal for them."For example, if a customer typically books every four weeks but has not booked for three months, that may indicate more than a simple delay. It could be an early sign that the relationship is weakening," said Sogbanmu."The goal is to give business owners greater visibility into customer stability so they can take action earlier, strengthen retention, and reduce the risk of losing valuable repeat business." StableKlin was born from a real-world problem Sogbanmu encountered while running Potteries Prime Cleaning, a cleaning business in the UK."As I worked with recurring customers, I noticed a pattern - customers rarely disappeared overnight. In most cases, there were subtle behavioural changes beforehand. Booking intervals became longer, routines became less consistent, and engagement gradually declined before revenue was affected," he said."I realised that while there were plenty of tools available to help manage operations, there were very few tools focused on understanding customer behaviour itself." That insight led him to begin developing StableKlin. Rather than focusing on operational management, the startup focuses on behavioural insight."It helps business owners identify changes in customer patterns before those changes become visible in revenue figures," said Sogbanmu. Currently self-funded, StableKlin has deliberately taken a lean approach to development, focusing on validating the core problem b

metadata.name: Nigeria's StableKlin is a behavioural intelligence platform designed for cleaning businesses

Additional information: id: 818e61adb012 title: How Zimbabwe's Maminda Agri-Fintech helps smallholders access finance, advisory, markets summary: Zimbabwean startup Maminda Agri-Fintech provides smallholder farmers with an integrated digital platform combining AI-powered farming support, digital financial services, satellite crop monitoring, cooperative financing, and market access tools. The bootstrapped startup is in pilot stage with fewer than 100 active users in Mashonaland and Manicaland, preparing for a pre-seed round and planning expansion to Zambia and Malawi. content: Zimbabwean startup Maminda Agri-Fintech is helping smallholder farmers access finance, agricultural advisory services and markets through a single digital ecosystem. Founded last year, Maminda Agri-Fintech combines AI-powered farming support, digital financial services, satellite crop monitoring, cooperative financing and market access tools designed specifically for underserved farming communities. Farmers can access personalised crop recommendations, monitor crop health using satellite data, participate in digital savings and financing groups through its Smart Digital Mukando system, build alternative credit profiles, and connect directly with buyers and input suppliers."Maminda operates across the entire agricultural value chain, supporting farmers from season planning and input financing through production, monitoring and post-harvest marketing," Maminda Edward Gandanzara, the startup' s founder and CEO, told Disrupt Africa. Smallholder farmers produce a significant proportion of Africa' s food, but remain among the most financially excluded groups. Many lack formal credit histories, limiting access to financing, quality inputs and profitable markets."Most existing solutions address only one challenge at a time, whether agronomic advisory, agricultural financing or market access. Maminda' s approach is to integrate these services into a single platform designed around the realities of smallholder farming," said Gandanzara. Competitors include agricultural financing and advisory platforms operating across Africa, such as Apollo Agriculture and other regional agri-fintech providers. In Zimbabwe, however, there are currently few platforms combining cooperative financing, AI-driven credit profiling, crop monitoring and marketplace services within a single ecosystem. The bootstrapped Maminda, which is preparing for a pre-seed fundraising round to support product development, pilot expansion and market growth, has won a host of awards already. Yet it is still at the pilot stage, working with a small group of farmers in Zimbabwe to validate product-market fit and refine the platform' s financial and agronomic models."The company currently has fewer than 100 active users. At this stage, the focus is on learning, validation and measurable farmer outcomes rather than rapid user acquisition," said Gandanzara. Maminda currently operates in Zimbabwe, with pilot activities concentrated in farming communitie

metadata.name: How Zimbabwe's Maminda Agri-Fintech helps smallholders access finance, advisory, markets

text:
Entity type: article
Additional information: id: 30829891268c title: Pan-African WRAPPED helping people find jobs in film industry summary: WRAPPED, a pan-African platform founded by Zoe Ramushu, centralizes job hiring in the film and television industry, replacing fragmented WhatsApp groups and spreadsheets. The startup gained 600 sign-ups in its first days and secured a partnership with MultiChoice. content: "WRAPPED is the app that I wish I had when I was trying to break into this industry. Right now, hiring in film and television is done through WhatsApp groups, Facebook groups, spreadsheets, and institutional memory captured in human brains," WRAPPED founder Zoe Ramushu tells Disrupt Africa."A message drops in a WhatsApp group, a gig is gone in two minutes. You didn' t even know what that job was. Two minutes." Ramushu has come up with a solution. What WRAPPED does is give the industry one centralised place where everybody is, where all the jobs are getting booked out of, at the click of a button."We' re categorised by skills and experience level so you find exactly who you' re looking for. We cut down 33 per cent of the pre-production workload. It sounds simple and it is, that' s the innovation," Ramushu said. She said the idea for WRAPPED came up based on her own experiences."I co-founded Swift (Sisters Working in Film and Television) and spent five-to-six years voluntarily building that organisation because I believe in being part of the change I' d like to see. Then I got my first Netflix project. Close to 120 people on set. I said to my line producer, we need at least one female in every department. Silence in the room. Like I was joking. We found them, a female in every single department. But it was an exercise," said Ramushu."Time-consuming. In production, time is literally money. That was the moment I said this cannot be the way things work. WRAPPED was real and existed in my mind from that moment. And glad today it' s now real for everyone else." The startup had 600 sign-ups in the first two to three days, with minimal marketing."That just spoke to the desire, the complete demand, for people working in this industry to have a centralised place to gather. A place that allows them to connect and be seen," said Ramushu."Our partnership with MultiChoice has been invaluable. They' ve been championing African stories for decades and having meaningful funding as well as their support has really just changed the shape of what we' re building." Ramushu said she doesn' t see WRAPPED as an African platform."It is built by Africans but it is a global platform. If it' s good tech, it' s good tech. If it' s a good product, it' s a good product. Same as film. Our presence at Berlin, at Cannes, three years running, those invitations are evidence that we are global. That' s not a plan. That' s already happening," she said. The platform is free for freelancers and agents, production companies can choose a subscription that best suits their needs. date: 2026-06-30 date_precision: day region: Afr

metadata.name: Pan-African WRAPPED helping people find jobs in film industry
